title: What I think about David Goggins -  Request

enrichment.summary: Kyle responds to a viewer request about David Goggins, endorsing Goggins' overtraining philosophy while arguing that physical training alone is incomplete without mental development — citing Plato's Laws on wedding mind and body training. He envisions a 'David Goggins 2.0' who combines athletic achievement with intellectual pursuits, advocates for transparent public records of both physical and mental accomplishments, and connects themes of competition, jealousy, societal openness, and the obsolescence of traditional schooling.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Favors overtraining over undertraining — the body heals and improves faster under constant stress
Respects his physical dedication but sees him as only halfway there — missing the mental/intellectual dimension
Physical and mental training must be combined, per Plato; neither alone makes a well-rounded person
Competition breeds realism, humility, and pragmatism; lack of competition breeds jealousy and ego
Strongly opposed — sees it as gatekept by arbitrary authority; wants transparent benchmarks instead
Dislikes the model of telling others how to think; wants everyone to share their own views rather than follow influencers
Supports them as necessary to free people to pursue self-improvement without fear
Uses him as an example of someone who cheats and lacks realistic self-assessment due to low competitive exposure
Criticizes steroid use as ego-driven and self-destructive
Closed, face-saving societies are worse; openness and transparency are superior

entities: Jack London (The Call of the Wild)

claims.subject: David Goggins
claims.claim: David Goggins is a motivational speaker who promotes hardcore exercise and the overtraining philosophy.

claims.subject: athletic training
claims.claim: There are two different philosophies with being an athlete: overtraining and undertraining.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle is on David Goggins' side, favoring overtraining.

claims.subject: training philosophy
claims.claim: A good analogy for overtraining is the way slaves, soldiers from ancient Rome, or sled dogs were trained by being worked to death, with the survivors becoming fit quickly.

claims.subject: training philosophy
claims.claim: The harder you train, the better you're going to be, as long as you don't get injured.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle tried to run 10 miles but decided against it because his muscles were still sore from lifting weights two days prior.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle lifted weights all day long two days ago and his muscles are still sore because he hadn't done it for months.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle rode five hours on his mountain bike the day after lifting weights, and his whole body was aching.

claims.subject: training philosophy
claims.claim: It's better to over-train than to under-train because the body heals itself and can reach a higher level of fitness quicker.

claims.subject: Arnold Schwarzenegger
claims.claim: Arnold Schwarzenegger said the same thing as David Goggins about feeling the burn during exercise.

claims.subject: sports
claims.claim: Sports make people the best athletes because they compete and push themselves as hard as they can.

claims.subject: competition
claims.claim: Competition is about trying to be the best you can be and using other people as benchmarks.

claims.subject: David Goggins
claims.claim: David Goggins ran 200 miles in a race and does pull-ups all day long.

claims.subject: Plato
claims.claim: Plato said in book Laws, book 7, chapter 14, that the training of the mind and the training of the body need to be wedded together like the horse and the rider.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle's life purpose is to marry mental and physical training to avoid wasting time with traditional education.

claims.subject: education
claims.claim: Traditional education is just sitting around and licking people's buttholes, and if you're not good at it or different, you get kicked out.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle got kicked out of college because a professor unfairly failed him, ruining his teaching license plans.

claims.subject: Spartans
claims.claim: The Spartans were a purely physically competitive society with 10,000 citizens who trained naked and competed in gymnastics, boxing, wrestling, and running.

claims.subject: Spartans
claims.claim: The Spartans had MMA-like sports in ancient Greece as part of the Olympics.

claims.subject: competition
claims.claim: The more competitive you are, the more realistic and less egotistical you become.

claims.subject: Alexander the Great
claims.claim: Alexander the Great chewed out a professional runner for letting him win a foot race because he knew it wasn't realistic for a king to be faster than a professional runner.

claims.subject: Alexander the Great
claims.claim: Alexander the Great said he would only race against other kings because it would be unfair to race against professional runners.

claims.subject: Dunning-Kruger effect
claims.claim: The Dunning-Kruger effect shows that the stupider you are, the smarter you think you are because you're not competing with your mind.

claims.subject: physical competition
claims.claim: The people who talk the most about being badass fighters are often the most physically incapable.

claims.subject: Donald Trump
claims.claim: Donald Trump claims to be the most persecuted, smartest, and most godly person in history because he doesn't know history.

claims.subject: competition
claims.claim: The least competitive people cheat the most and are the most jealous.

claims.subject: Arnold Schwarzenegger
claims.claim: Arnold Schwarzenegger was always super supportive of his bodybuilder buddies.

claims.subject: Lance Armstrong
claims.claim: Lance Armstrong is an example of someone who won by cheating and then tried to drag his competitors down.

claims.subject: future society
claims.claim: In the future, everyone should have a YouTube channel to share their thoughts and accomplishments transparently.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle wants to create a daily vlog show produced live from his bedroom studio.

claims.subject: YouTube
claims.claim: YouTube should be a platform where everyone shares their thoughts and accomplishments, not just influencers.

claims.subject: jealousy
claims.claim: Jealousy was a big problem in ancient Roman and Greek times, leading to poisonings and other harms.

claims.subject: narcissists
claims.claim: Narcissists use your confidences against you and focus only on your negatives.

claims.subject: China
claims.claim: China's closed society leads to people worrying about saving face and making up stories, like the president claiming to be an endurance athlete.

claims.subject: David Goggins
claims.claim: David Goggins' approach is cool, but Kyle doesn't understand why people need motivation to exercise.

claims.subject: steroids
claims.claim: People use steroids to get physically strong quickly but ruin their health and die young.

claims.subject: time management
claims.claim: The key to exercise is having spare time, which requires streamlining society and eliminating extraneous expenses like traditional education.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle was kicked out of college because a professor lied to him and ruined his teaching license plans.

claims.subject: transparency
claims.claim: Society needs transparency so no one can get in your way but yourself.

claims.subject: David Goggins
claims.claim: Kyle would love to know David Goggins' physical capabilities, like his bench press or biking endurance.

claims.subject: future fitness
claims.claim: The next step after David Goggins is combining physical and mental training, like riding 100 miles while listening to a book and then reviewing it.

claims.subject: social safety nets
claims.claim: There should be scholarships or government support for people to take time off work for self-improvement.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle feels it's a bummer that David Goggins makes money from motivational speaking instead of using that time to exercise.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle had insomnia last night, likely due to recovering from new exercise routines.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle thinks people don't support him because they're jealous of his spare time to build his website.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle wants to be like David Goggins but with brains, combining physical and mental achievements.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle needs to pass his history tests before asking for money from educational foundations.

claims.subject: future media
claims.claim: In the future, Kyle envisions making videos of different lengths to explain his ideas, from 30 minutes to 10 seconds.

claims.subject: sports popularity
claims.claim: When Kyle was growing up, only boxing, football, baseball, and basketball were popular, but now endurance sports like running are mainstream.

claims.subject: social media
claims.claim: Social media has made young people, especially girls, more muscular and physically fit than ever.

claims.subject: David Goggins
claims.claim: David Goggins is important because he changed from being a fat dude to a fit motivational figure.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle can only do 50-mile mountain bike rides because his stomach can't handle eating during rides.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle threw up during his last bike ride because his stomach couldn't handle water.

claims.subject: future categories
claims.claim: The ultimate category in the future should be the master of all trades, combining physical and mental training.

claims.subject: Plato
claims.claim: Plato's idea of wedding mind and body training can now be achieved with modern technology.

claims.subject: future society
claims.claim: Society should use technology to combine physical and mental training, shut down schools, and streamline the economy for social safety nets.

transcript.text: Hey, here's a request video about what I think about David Goggins from K9365.
Just did a request about Warhammer 40,000, and now he wants to know about David Goggins.
So David Goggins' deal, he's the guy who, he's that guy, who, like, motivates.
He's the motivational speaker and does the, that you need exercise, super hardcore.
I guess he's into, like, the overtraining, the overtraining philosophy.
There's, like, two different philosophies with, like, being an athlete.
There's overtraining and then undertraining.
I'm actually on David Goggins' side, overtraining.
A good analogy would be, like, there's that book, The Call of the Wild.
I was listening to the audio book, like, a long time ago for a bit of it.
And, like, yeah, like, the way that you would get, like, a slave in shape or maybe a soldier in an army from ancient Rome in shape
Or, like, a sled dog in shape would be to just throw them in the sled dog and then just work them to death.
And some of them maybe get worked to death and don't survive.
But the ones that survive get fit really fast, really strong.
Like, what doesn't kill you makes you stronger, you know?
As long as you don't get an injury, then the harder you train, the better you're going to be.
And, yeah, I kind of believe in, I like that approach better.
Like, yesterday I was going to try to run 10 miles, but I decided against it because I was still recovering from, my muscles were still sore.
I lifted weights all day long two days ago, and my muscles are still sore from that because I haven't done it for, like, months
Because I've been too busy sitting on my apartment.
And then the next day I rode five hours on my mountain bike.
And as I was riding back, like, my whole body was just aching because all my muscles were sore from lifting weights,
But I was also riding.
And so my back and everything was hurting, and I wasn't used to that.
And, you know, it wasn't so bad that it was, like, not fun.
It wasn't overdoing it.
But then yesterday I tried to go, I was going to go on the run, but I decided against it
Because I could tell that if I was going to actually do the run, I was going to go really slow and not do very well.
But in the long run, like, if I had it gone on the run, I'd be better off now.
Because it's better to – I think it's better to over-train than to under-train
Because the body – when the body is constantly trying to heal itself, it'll – it just – it just –
You can get to a higher level of fitness quicker than if you're just kind of take it the easy way.
But, yeah, so, like, that whole motivation stuff, like, you know, when it hurts, you get better.
Or, like, Arnold Schwarzenegger said the same exact thing that David Goggin said.
He's like, you feel the burn, you know, and the more you feel the burn, the better you're going to do.
And, you know, that's why sports makes people the best athletes because they're competing
And they're going as hard as they possibly can as they're competing.
And the more you compete, going as hard as you can, the better you're going to be, you know.
Like, if you're in a race with other people, you just push yourself harder
Because you just have that natural instinct to try to, like, stay in front of somebody.
You know, like, if you're on a bike ride and you pass somebody, you know that the person's behind you
And then you make sure to keep running hard because you want to be consistent.
You don't want to, like, be like you worked harder to pass them and then you let off.
I think it's the same thing with, like, running or, like, ego lifting.
If, you know, somebody's watching you or something, it's easy to, like, film.
Push yourself harder if you're filming.
And, yeah, so I think that's a cool approach, you know, and I like that he does all the different sports.
I guess he ran 200 miles in a race and he does, like, pull-ups all day long and that kind of stuff.
That's fun.
And that's, but I think it's only half of it.
Plato, in book Laws, book 7, chapter 14, I think it was, Plato said,
The training of the mind and the training of the body need to be wedded together like the horse and the rider.
So you would never train one without the other because if you do one too much, then you're not, you're not a well-rounded person.
You'll develop problems, you know, like you won't live long enough if you don't exercise and then you'll develop, like, emotional problems or whatever.
And then your brain won't get enough blood flow and your brain power will go down.
But if you train too much physically, then your brain will suffer.
That's a great quote, and that's kind of what my whole life purpose is, is to marry the two and so that you don't have to waste your time with college and schools.
Because that's just sitting around twiddling thumb and then licking people's buttholes.
And if you're not good enough licking butthole, then you get kicked out like me.
Or if you're different, you get kicked out.
Just putting way too much power in the hands of these asshole people like Fort Loser College and even in high school and everything.
Just provide, like, benchmarks to go after and then go after the benchmark, but not just physical.
So I think the next level would be, yeah, to incorporate physical.
I think, I was David Goggins, I think, I think, I don't know, I should check out his website.
But, I mean, everything should be, people's accomplishments need to be as, like, readily available as absolutely, as, like, humanly possible.
So, like, if you have a page of, like, all of your, like, records or whatever, just have them all on one page.
So somebody can go there and they can see it and then if they click it, it takes you to the YouTube channel of them doing it.
And then you can see it and then you can compete, you know.
And the more competitive we all are, the more, the less, like, egotistical we are and the more realistic we are.
I just read book one of Plutarch's Moralia, Morals, and three of those chapters are sayings, laconic sayings, sayings of the Spartans.
And the Spartans were, like, a purely physically competitive society.
They didn't do any work because they had the helix, slave helix, do all their work for them.
So their jobs, everybody's job in Sparta, and they even kind of kept their numbers to about 10,000 evenly throughout their entire existence because if it was too much, you'd get too unwieldy.

But 10,000 citizens was all they needed because back in those days when you'd hit the field, you know, like, two legions, 10,000 people was enough of an army to defeat anybody because that's about as big of an army as you'd ever go up against.
And if you were outnumbered, you could kick their asses anyway because you're all professional, like, fighters.
And so, yeah, that's all they did, men and women.
They would train naked, and they'd just train all day long and compete.
They'd do gymnastics.
They had boxing, wrestling, and then boxing, wrestling mixed.
So they had MMA back in ancient Greece and Sparta.
I don't know how much of the wrestling incorporated, like, choke holds and arm locks and all that kind of stuff, but it probably did.
So they had full-on MMA back in those days as part of, like, an Olympic sport.
But, yeah, and then they did running and all that kind of stuff.
But, you know, the more competitive you are, the more realistic you are because they would, you know, like, there's a saying, like, there's a Spartan woman's son got killed.
And they're like, oh, no, you know, he was the greatest Spartan.
And she's like, no, there's many Spartans better than him.
Um, she was just realistic.
But he's still a great man because he's a Spartan.
Um, and, like, Alexander the Great, like, he, uh, he did a foot race with a professional runner and the professional runner was letting him win.

And he chewed out the professional runner for letting him win because he knew it wasn't realistic for the leader, for the king to be faster than the professional runner because he thought pragmatically.
So the more, more, the more competitive you are, the more pragmatic you are and the less, the less, like, you know, fantastical you are.
Alexander also said, like, his father was like, yeah, you're a good runner.
You should join the Olympics.

And he's like, well, if there was a, if there was a category where I raced against other kings, because he knew it would be unfair for him to race against in the Olympics against professional runners because they were all running all day long.
He was a king doing administrative work, so he needed a new division, and he was, like, realistic about that.

The people who are the least realistic about, like, violence and physical competition are the people who are the least exposed to it, um, who least, least, um, and I think it goes the same way with minds.
It's like the Dunning-Kruger effect.
The stupider you are, like, the more, the smarter you think you are compared to everybody because you're, you're not in the world of competing with your mind.
You're isolated, and you're only comparing yourself with other stupid people.
It's the same thing with physicality.
Like, the people who I've seen the most talk like they're badass fighters are the most physically incapable people.
Like, I knew a kid, I won't drop any names, but super skinny dude, like 150 pounds, couldn't bench press 130 pounds one time to save his life.

Like, going around talking like he was a total badass and, you know, and, like, like, violence and, you know, like, once he was, like, he was, like, my roommate, and he was, like, he, like, snuck in the back.

He's, like, if you were, I was in such a bad mood, if you were here, you'd be dead right now, and I was, like, and then there was this big fat dude who, like, I knew, who, like, was talking like he was this badass fighter and stuff, and it's, like, he could barely walk, you know?

So it's, like, but it's the same thing with the mind, you know, like, Donald Trump, all the time, he's, like, he's the most persecuted guy ever in the history of humankind because he's the smartest guy ever in the history of humankind, and he's the most godly and all that kind of stuff, and it was all of his enemies are the worst in humankind because he doesn't know history, so he's, of course, he's the most ever.
So the more, the more competitive, and, like, the more, the more, the least competitive you are, the more jealous you get, and the less you understand what competition is.

Like, there was this kid named, maybe I won't say his name, but this kid, like, who was in my class in fourth grade, and I was a bike racer in fourth grade, and he came over to my house to hang out, and he had a bike, and we were, like, okay, let's go on a ride.
He's, like, oh, no, I don't want to go on a ride because he was, like, didn't have the fitness to go on an actual ride.
So I was, like, okay, well, let's do a little race around the block.
So we did a little race around the block, but, like, I went around the first corner, and I slipped my chain.
I threw my chain, which is something that never, ever, ever happens.
So then he went around the block, and I was, like, hey, we needed the race again.
He's, like, oh, no, I won.
I won the race.
I won.
He's running around, like, I beat Kyle.
I beat Kyle.
I beat him.
Like, no, I threw my chain.
That wasn't a competition.
So then they switch it into cheating.
So then, like, Donald Trump has to cheat to become the president.
I think he cheated because he won all the swing states, which never happens.
And then the swing states he didn't win, they, like, they were going up.
And then all of a sudden they spiked, and, yeah, the people who are the least competitive cheat the most.
And the people who are the most competitive, competition, all competition is, is, like, trying to be the best you can be
And getting motivated by looking around you and seeing, like, using other people as benchmarks
And yourself as a benchmark is part of the entertainment of getting better and the best you can be.
And the most, you know, like, the most competitive people are the most supportive of their competitors.
Like Arnold Schwarzenegger, they said he was always super supportive of all of his bodybuilder buddies.
And, yeah, the people who cheat the most are the most, like, are the most, and then win by cheating
Are kind of the most, like, biggest assholes, you know, like Lance Armstrong.
And they try to, like, they think that part of the competition is, like, dragging your competitors down
And hurting their name and all that kind of a stuff.
And, yeah, so if everybody were to, like, listen to David Goggins and just, like, get motivated and exercise, that'd be cool.
But, like, I think you need to record all of your feats.
So that's why I think you should record your mental and physical, because if you try to be as well-rounded of a person as you can,
It's like Alexander says, you're never going to be the best at one thing.
But there's nothing wrong.
Like, in the past, there's that saying, Jack of all trades, master of none.
And you don't want to be that guy, because then you'll never get a job, because people will only hire the master.
And so only people who are focused on one thing.
And so the only people who could be, like, a renaissance man, polymath, would be, like, the king, you know,
Like one person who had the money to get away with it, like, with their fuck you money.
And so, like, you know, like, in the past, like, if you're, like, different and poor, you're a weirdo.
But if you're different and rich, you're eccentric, because you, because, like, and that's why, like, a lot of rich people in the past, like, would just waste their money,
Because they would try stuff that's risky to try to change the world, and a lot of times it would fail.
But it didn't really matter, because they survived anyway.
And, but I think that in the future, you know, now, when everybody can be, like, rich, you know, everybody can get a second chance,
Especially when you have a bunch of social safety nets, people can, you know, take risk and try to do stuff and fail and win and fail.
But you can, you can try to, you know, like, it develops new categories, new, new, more and more and more categories.
And until eventually, then the new category, which is, turns out to be the best category, is just the jack of all trades category.
Like, who's the best at all of it?
And so, yeah, in the future, I think we should wed mental with physical.
And so, like, that's what I'm trying to do now.
Like, like, I don't, I wouldn't, if I was just, like, a, got a really good athlete, like a triathlete or something,
And I got really good at that, and, like, the best in the world, I wouldn't be satisfied.
I'd feel like an idiot, because then I'd look at some people who'd smart, and I'd be like, oh, no, but they're changing the world, and I'm just being selfish.
And I think a lot of people, like, athletes kind of could feel, like, guilt sometimes,
Especially people, athletes who grew up with money, and they could have done anything,
And they decided to blow all their money becoming a better athlete or traveling around the world or something.
Instead of, like, trying to save the whales or something like that.
So in the future, I think, in order to help with that, you know, guilt and just to be able to motivate to be the best you can be is to incorporate the two.
And so that's why I designed my website.
It's perfectly appropriate for my website.
Like, I think David Goggins 2.0 would be, like, the guy who is, like, look what I did here, physical this, physical this, physical, but look what I did mental while I did the physical.
So, like, that's what I'm trying to do now, trying to get back in shape right now.
But I want to pass my history test where I memorized 150 different things from England, 1500 to 2000, from recognizing images.
I'll put the link below to the test.
But also random number generator test for AD, BC, ancient history, and 20th century.
Yeah, and then you just tally up the scores of all of those things.
And then the book reviews, like I'm doing Morale right now, all up on YouTube.
That's what YouTube should be for everybody to do that stuff.
Because, like, I'm watching, like, I was watching Secular Talk the other day.
I think I'm subscribed to him, and I never really used to listen to that much, Kyle Kalinske.
And he's cool.
He's online with me physically, I mean, politically.
But as I'm watching him talk, and, like, Hassan Hassanabi, the Cenk Uygur's nephew up there, it's kind of the same thing.
A lot of these people, they make a bunch of money on YouTube.
But basically what they're doing, because that's what I was thinking when I was watching Kyle Kalinske.
Basically what they're doing is they're just doing, like, they're doing, like, a What I Think About the World vlog.
And that's great.
But they're making money on it.
But what they're doing is they're telling everybody else how to think.
But the assumption of telling everybody else how to think is that everybody else needs to be taught how to think.
And so what I envision is that everybody is like a Kyle Kalinske and a Hassan, just up there on YouTube every day,
Saying what they think about stuff.
And they have the little clips, and they don't have, and they have, they don't have people.
Nobody has somebody working for you.
You do it yourself.
I have a studio in my bedroom that I'm going to, whenever I get around to it, I'm going to set up.
And I want to have, like, a daily vlog show where I hit the buttons and I, and I have, like, a perfectly, like, studio,
A perfectly organized studio that, like, perfectly produced, just like it's CNN, you know.
But I don't put any time into it because it's all live, and it's all done.
All the editing is done live while you're doing your video, and then you just upload it.
And so, like, everybody does that, right?
But if everybody does that, you don't make money doing that because people don't watch everybody.
You don't sit around watching other people, you know.
The reason that those guys make money doing that is because nobody makes videos.
Imagine if everybody did a video like that, you wouldn't have the time to watch somebody else, you know.
So I do it because people want, like, want to be told how to think.
And they also, like, they're also, like, there's too much us versus them, left versus right.
So then it's like they're watching them to be on their side, and then the other people watch the Daily Wire to be on the Daily Wire side
So they can fight it out and who's more powerful.
But in the future, like, when everybody is more educated and more, like, there's not the left and the right fighting each other
And just a bunch of people trying to learn things, then it'd be a bunch of people doing the YouTube videos
And talking about stuff, not to make money, but just to share themselves and to make themselves better communicator
And let people know about themselves and then to, like, and then, but not just talk about politics,
But, like, every day, like, teaching the world.
Because if you want to go out and teach the stuff, instead of teaching them, like, the right way to think
Or, like, what side to be on politically or explaining stuff, talking about other people
And, like, you know, you're, like, a thought guru or whatever, instead of that, you're actually teaching an actual thing.
Like, I read this book. Let me tell you what I thought about the book, you know?
So if somebody wants to, is tired of listening to the book, like, text-to-speech reading it to them
Or some person reading out the book, you want to hear what somebody from a certain perspective thinks about that book
Or thinks about that subject.
And then you just kind of, people pop up and you listen to this guy's perspective,
But nobody actually follows anybody.
I saw a title of a YouTube video, like, the age of, like, influencers is over,
And maybe that's where it's kind of trending now.
Like, it's not so much like you becoming a fan of somebody and watching all their stuff
Because there's so many people out there.
It's more like you watching a video of a video that you want to watch,
Like a certain type of video, and it happens to be a person,
And maybe you see a bunch of videos from the same person, but it's mainly about that.
And so in the future, you know, maybe we'll blow up even more
In that everybody will have a YouTube channel, and then everybody will think something, you know?
And so maybe in your life you would only watch one video from one person,
And, like, the person you watch the most videos of would be you've only watched 50 videos of that person
Because everybody does videos, and you're actually doing your videos all the time, you know?
So that's how I think we can wed, like, training of your mind and your body.
Like, what I want to do is, like, you know, do a, like, the last few bike rides I did,
I was listening to Plutarch Morale.
It's like a 1,335-page book.
It's huge, and I was listening to it, like, on regular speed.
But as soon as I get done with it, I'm halfway through.
I'm done with book one.
I want to do the YouTube video, you know, and I talk about what I did in the video,
And then I talk, and then I do the links of my Strava and what I did on the ride,
And you compare yourself with other people, but you don't get overly competitive, you know,
Because you always know that there's no way you can be the best at everything all the time.
Like, I went mountain biking the other day, and I think I was, like, 1,400th place on the downhill,
But, like, there was no way I was going to be much better than that because it was Sunday, you know?
Like, it was packed with people.
I had to get off the trail every, like, 100 meters to let somebody pass.
